ci-local.hooksPathNote: install.sh computes the actual path via realpath; downstream projects that copy ci-local/ to .ci-local/ will see '.ci-local/hooks' here
ci-local.description: Local Docker-based CI replacement for GitHub Actions

security-baseline.description: Regression gate: blocks PRs that introduce NEW vulns vs baseline

ai-attribution-blocker.description: Best-effort commit-msg hook with Unicode normalization. NOT an adversarial security control.
ai-attribution-blocker.threatModel: accidental copy-paste of AI tool output; whitespace/markdown/emoji tricks; combining diacritics; common Unicode invisibles
outOfScope: bare provider name without verb+preposition context (high false-positive risk)
ai-attribution-blocker.recommendation: for adversarial threat models, use signed commits + human review

supply-chain.description: Caret-bounded overrides for known-vulnerable transitive deps

self-dogfooding.description: javi-forge runs its own tools against itself in CI

findingsAddressed: missing javi-forge CLI prereq check in install.sh
tmp-file leak in test suite (added trap EXIT)
knownLimitations: commit-msg blocker: cross-script homoglyphs, tag chars, variation selectors not detected — see modules.ai-attribution-blocker.outOfScope

ci-local/install.{sh,ps1}: install-time check only — between install and the next commit the PATH could be remapped or the binary replaced. Mitigated by symlink-path warnings, readlink/ResolveLinkTarget chain resolution, refusal to wire when the resolved binary is under user-writable caches, and (on Windows) icacls hardening of the hooks directory. Remaining risk is the responsibility of the user's file-permission setup.

ci-local PowerShell port: requires pwsh 7.2+ (FileSystemInfo.ResolveLinkTarget). Validated on Linux pwsh 7.6 (detect mode + symlink-traversal PoC). Quick/shell/full modes not yet smoke-tested on a real Windows host — needs validation before tagging a release.

ci-local PowerShell port: lib/common.psm1 is verified to resolve INSIDE the project root before Import-Module loads it; module signing is NOT verified. Users running install.ps1 from a fresh clone of an untrusted source are out of scope.
lib/common.psm1: detect_framework() function from common.sh is not ported (the bash version has no callers and is effectively dead code).

ci-local installers trust the host PATH: a malicious 'realpath' (bash) or shadowed Get-Item provider (PowerShell) earlier on PATH/PSModulePath defeats the symlink checks. Out of scope: an attacker with write access to the user's $PATH, $PROFILE, or $PSModulePath already has user-level RCE before the installer runs. Mitigation: defense-in-depth — install.ps1 strips any pre-existing Resolve-RealPath alias/function and verifies the binding resolves to its own script file before use.

ci-local installers trust the lib content at install time: a non-symlink direct overwrite of lib/common.{sh,psm1} bypasses the symlink check. Out of scope: an attacker with write access to lib/ has user-level RCE through any future invocation. Recommended mitigation: keep lib/ under version control, run 'git diff lib/' before commits, and on shared systems set restrictive file permissions on the lib directory.
